content:
Hey everyone,

for my upcoming master thesis (or as part of it) I wanted to work on a Rust-based Linux Security Module (LSM), so I thought before I go ahead and start that I'd ask here for a few opinions :)

From my brief look into LSMs and the Rust kernel module registration code, it seems like LSM registration would be somewhat similar, mostly with different #[link_section] attributes. I also saw that @Alice Ryhl has sent some patches for bindings to the security/LSM header for Rust Binder, but those look not very LSM specific yet, so these bindings would also be part of the thesis for me.

Do you think creating (not yet production ready) bindings and a very basic Rust LSM on top of them would be feasible as part of a thesis, or is the Rust support in the kernel not there yet? Is anyone already working on LSM bindings?

Thank you!

Maybe as a quick clarification, I wasn't looking for an in-depth analysis of the issues I might run into, that's part of the thesis work. I was only looking for a quick sanity check that this isn't a really dumb idea because there are way too many things missing. Since no one told me to please not do this, I guess I can interpret this as it not being all that bad :D

Also, I do plan to publish what I achieve afterwards, even if eventual upstreaming is pretty unlikely for a while since an actual Rust-based LSM in the kernel as user of the bindings does not look like something happening soon.
Yeah I'm not hoping for that to happen anytime soon
I don't know much about what makes LSM special

From my brief look into them, they're registered to the .lsm_info.init section instead of .init.text like normal kernel modules (at least that's what I saw in the Rust kernel module proc macro), and then there are a few other macros to initialize the struct containing the functions that should be called for the hooks.
Seems doable is also what I thought, and I figured if everything else fails I can still do the LSM registration in C and then call into Rust for the actual logic, but I'd like to avoid that.
It's unfortunate if you're going to spend time on something you're not planning to upstream, though
Though even if you had a user, a master's thesis is not very long time.

I don't mind upstreaming it, I just don't see it happening very soon and I don't think the resulting LSM from the thesis will be worth upstreaming since it's purpose is rather as a test bed and for further research.

Alice Ryhl schrieb:

Though even if you had a user, a master's thesis is not very long time.

Yeah, that as well, I don't think the end result will be polished enough at the end of the thesis. If there is interest though, I don't mind continuing to work on it.

It is nice to see a MSc thesis on Rust for Linux!

I agree that "landing it upstream" as a concrete thesis goal is something that I would generally recommend avoiding, since it depends on whether there is an actual user needing it, on upstream maintainers, etc., unless you have talked to the maintainers first and everyone is in agreement. Just "submitting to the LKML as an RFC" is a much safer goal, if someone needs one or wants to be involved.

Hey, quick update for this topic: I have handed in my thesis a month ago and successfully defended it today, receiving highest marks for both. I'm looking into publishing the thesis in case anyone is curious to check it out (the university offers this, where it also gets a DOI and everything), but I'm not sure there is much to gain from it for general Rust kernel development, as the topic focuses more on the security policy execution and the semantics for the kinds of policies I focus on.

Thank you everyone for your help with my questions, especially @Boqun Feng for the WIP RCU bindings and @Fiona Behrens for the quick feedback on my LSM "bindings"!

I will post the thesis when it's public.
It's a real-world example proving the RCU protected pointer works (at least for global locks), so thank you!

Hello, very late update from me, my thesis was finally published last week, it's available at https://doi.org/10.22032/dbt.66733. Code and artifacts are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17765425, but more conveniently at https://github.com/o1oo11oo/thesis-code (supporting code) and https://github.com/o1oo11oo/linux/tree/thesis-main (kernel code).

I wanted to fix some typos and oversights before publication, which was allowed, but then I was busy with other things, so even just going through everything once took quite a while unfortunately.

I'm not sure how useful this will be for anyone, as the thesis focuses on dynamic security policies a lot more than the concrete Rust implementation of them, but maybe this is helpful or interesting to someone. I'm not sure if it still makes sense to send this to a mailing list, maybe Rust-for-Linux and LSM? Feedback on this appreciated. The bindings are still in the same state as they were when I sent them to the lists for feedback, and I'm not currently working on polishing them.

The code is as used during the thesis, and especially during the end when I didn't have that much time left I might have cut some corners :) Rebasing this to a more recent kernel version likely requires cleaning that up, but there shouldn't be that much.

Thanks again for all the help :)
